text: The surgeon incises the peritoneum, creating a relatively large opening around the deep ring.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to dissect tissue, but quickly transitions to a short dissection technique to minimize thermal spread and avoid injury to nearby vasculature and nerves.
The surgeon demonstrates the abandonment of the sac, noting that the incision around the sac is not completely circular.
The surgeon cuts easily accessible tissue to facilitate identification during the creation of the peritoneal flap.
The surgeon continues the dissection in the parietal compartment, extending the flap development down towards the pubis.
The surgeon begins the lateral dissection in the visceral compartment, carefully maneuvering around the visible anatomical structures.
The surgeon approaches the previously made fenestrations in the tissue, preparing to navigate through these openings during the dissection.
The surgeon encounters the previously made fenestration and begins finalizing the circumferential incision around the neck and deep ring.
The surgeon demonstrates the parietalization or lateralization of the vessels, ensuring that only the peritoneum is included in the dissection field.
The surgeon completes the circumferential incision around the sac, ensuring precise separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon has completely dissociated the peritoneum from the distal sac, revealing the separated anatomical structures.
The surgeon transitions to a standard inguinal hernia repair, focusing on the parietalization of the cord elements, ensuring they are adequately separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon parietalizes the vas deferens, creating an adequate pocket in the tissue for mesh placement.
The surgeon performs dissection in the medial area to achieve a clear view of the myopectenial orifice, ensuring proper exposure of the anatomical structures.

The surgeon continues parietalization of the vas deferens, carefully navigating around prominent vessels near the obturator region. The lateral dissection is finalized to create space for the placement of an extra-large mesh.
The surgical field shows more oozing than usual, with a visible hole in the tissue. The surgeon avoids major dissection around the cord elements.
The surgeon places an extra large mesh into the prepared space, ensuring it fits well within the surgical pocket created during the procedure.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap using a standard technique for peritoneal incision closure, addressing a fenestration around the area of the hernia neck.
The surgeon exposes the hernia neck, showing no tension in the area. The peritoneal flap closure is completed under 15 millimeters of mercury pressure.
The surgical field shows a flap closure with no visible tension, indicating a successful procedure.
